draft.introduction: Democritus of Abdera (c. 460–370 BCE) developed the atomic theory first proposed by his teacher Leucippus. These key fragments establish that only atoms and void truly exist, and that sensory qualities are merely convention.

translation.original_ref: DK 68 B9 (Sextus, Adv. Math. VII.135); B125 (Galen, De Med. Empir.)
translation.text: By convention sweet, by convention bitter, by convention hot, by convention cold, by convention color; but in reality atoms and void. In truth we know nothing, for truth is in the depths.
translation.original_text: νόμῳ γλυκύ, νόμῳ πικρόν, νόμῳ θερμόν, νόμῳ ψυχρόν, νόμῳ χροιή· ἐτεῆ δὲ ἄτομα καὶ κενόν

translator_notes.note: The distinction between nomos (convention, νόμῳ) and etei (in reality, ἐτεῆ) anticipates the modern distinction between primary and secondary qualities. Epicurus later built his entire physics on this Democritean foundation.
